symptoms.symptom_name: Chronic dull fronto-occipital headache for six months

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Chronic headache from dural stretch by an enlarging sellar mass.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute severe (thunderclap-like) escalation of the headache

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Abrupt severe headache change signals possible acute haemorrhage/infarction into the adenoma.
Thunderclap headache mandates exclusion of SAH by urgent imaging.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid bilateral outer-field (bitemporal) visual loss as patient-reported tunnel vision

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Bitemporal field loss localises specifically to the optic chiasm.
A fixed dense bilateral defect is not a transient scintillating aura.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dense objective bitemporal hemianopsia on formal confrontation testing, respecting the vertical midline

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Objective midline-respecting bitemporal defect confirms a chiasmal lesion — a sealed clinician measurement the AI Doctor cannot obtain remotely.

symptoms.symptom_name: One-year loss of libido and erectile dysfunction (secondary hypogonadism)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Central hypogonadism from pituitary compression of gonadotroph function.
Anterior pituitary hormone loss indicates a chronic sellar mass.

symptoms.symptom_name: Profound fatigue, cold intolerance and pale skin (secondary hypothyroidism / hypopituitarism)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Central hypothyroidism and cortisol deficiency produce fatigue, cold intolerance and pallor.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pale, thin skin and loss of axillary hair on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Loss of androgen-dependent body hair and skin changes reflect chronic hormone deficiency — a clinician examination finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Cranial nerves III, IV and VI intact; pupils equal and reactive

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Intact ocular motor nerves argue against cavernous sinus invasion — a clinician exam finding, sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: No vomiting, no fever, no neck stiffness

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of meningism lowers (but does not exclude) meningitic/SAH causes; imaging still required.

symptoms.symptom_name: Intrasellar mass ± haemorrhage on MRI pituitary/brain

investigation_details.expected_result: Intrasellar/suprasellar macroadenoma compressing the optic chiasm; possible haemorrhage/infarction indicating apoplexy
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Missed apoplexy and chiasmal compression; irreversible visual loss.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Imaging defines the mass and any haemorrhage — hospital-based, never available in real-time telehealth.

symptoms.symptom_name: Pituitary hormone panel showing low cortisol, low free T4/TSH, low testosterone (± prolactin change)

investigation_details.investigation_name: Anterior pituitary hormone panel and 9am cortisol
investigation_details.expected_result: Pattern of anterior hypopituitarism with cortisol deficiency
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Unrecognised adrenal insufficiency risks fatal crisis during apoplexy.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Biochemical confirmation of anterior pituitary failure — venous bloods, sealed and telehealth-inaccessible.

symptoms.symptom_name: Clinician-measured blood pressure 115/75 with no postural drop currently

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Currently normotensive without postural drop, but cortisol-deficient shock can develop rapidly — a clinician measurement, sealed.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: Pituitary macroadenoma with optic chiasm compression
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Bitemporal visual loss + chronic-then-acute headache + hormone hyper/hyposecretion + intrasellar mass on MRI is the classic macroadenoma constellation.
Hypogonadism, hypothyroid features, skin/hair changes and biochemical hypopituitarism flag cortisol-deficiency danger during apoplexy.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Defines the sellar mass, chiasmal compression, and any haemorrhage indicating apoplexy — arranged via emergency transfer, not remotely.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Anterior pituitary hormone panel and cortisol
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Detects secondary adrenal insufficiency requiring urgent stress-dose steroids in hospital.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Formal visual-field assessment (perimetry) and ophthalmology review
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Quantifies and monitors the chiasmal field defect; guides urgency of decompression.
Hyponatraemia is common in hypopituitarism/apoplexy and needs correction.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Non-contrast CT head only, as a substitute for MRI, then routine follow-up
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: CT alone can miss a small apoplexy/adenoma; downgrading to routine follow-up would under-manage a sight- and life-threatening emergency.
